coredata.dc:title: A yellow-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing of vitamin B12 and their cell-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Zishan
Chen, Zikang
Luo, Jiabao
Zhu, Zhenpeng
Zhang, Xinguo
Liu, Ruiyuan
Wu, Zhan-chao
coredata.dc:description: A simple approach for the synthesis of novel yellow luminescent carbon dots (Y-CDs) has been developed via solvothermal method using l-tartaric acid and urea as precursors. The obtained Y-CDs were found to emit bright yellow luminescence under UV illumination with a quantum yield of 16.7%. Meanwhile, the Y-CDs exhibited excitation-dependence, photo-stability, and well dispersibility. The Y-CDs efficiently and selectively detected the VB12 in aqueous solution through a fluorescent “Turn Off” phenomenon. Excellent linear relationship between Y-CDs emission ratio and VB12 concentration from 0 μM to 200 μM is observed, in which the correlation coefficient is 0.997 and the detection limit is 2.045 μmol/L. The quenching mechanism was investigated through proper microscopic and spectroscopic studies. In a parallel experiment, the Y-CDs were used as a cell-imaging probe for MDA-MB-231 cells. Our results demonstrated that as-obtained Y-CDs can be simply used as versatile probes, highlighting their potential in different biological studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Luminescence
VB12 detection
Sensing mechanism
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the yellow-emitting carbon dots (Y-CDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The wide scan XPS spectrum of Y-CDs (a), High resolution XPS of C 1s (b), O 1s (c) and N 1s (d) spectra of the Y-CDs, FT-IR spectra of Y-CDs (e).

XRD diffraction pattern of Y-CDs (a), with the TEM image of Y-CDs (b). Absorption, excitation and emission spectra (c) and CIE coordinates of Y-CDs (d). PL spectra of Y-CDs excited at different wavelengths (e) and excitation-emission matrix of Y-CDs solution (f).
Emission spectra (a) and CIE coordinates (b) of different concentrations of Y-CDs (a) with the corresponding photographs at 450 nm excitation (c).

Fluorescence responses of the Y-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of VB12 (λ ex = 450 nm, λ em = 550 nm) the inset shows the corresponding photographic images with the concentration of 0 μM VB12 and 200 μM VB12 respectively under 395 nm UV illumination (a) and the relationship between the emission integrated area and the concentration of VB12 (b).
An overlap of VB12 absorption spectrum and Y-CDs emission spectrum (a) and time-resolved decay of the Y-CDs in the presence and absence of VB12. The inset table presents the lifetime value (b).
The UV–Vis absorption spectra of a variety of vitamins and excitation and PL spectra of Y-CDs (a) and fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs in the presence of different 200 μM vitamins (b).

(a) Cytotoxicity effect of Y-CDs on MDA-MB-231 pancreatic cancer cells. (b and c) Microscope images of MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with 20 mg/mL Y-CDs for 1 h and 4 h. The left panels are bright field images of the cancer cells, the middle panels are the fluorescence images, and the last panel is the merged image of the bright field and fluorescent images.
Comparison of different methods for detecting VB12.

row.entry: High sensitivity, low detection limit and simple equipment requirements.
Time-consuming, laborious, inaccurate, complex, low selectivity, expertise and experience required. .
Low sensitivity and higher detection limit.
Rapid, sensitive, strong separation, accurate measurement and it is easy to combine with other technologies and need the small number of samples.
Complicated operation and professional training required.
Simple operation, rapid reaction, no reaction time, no interference from other vitamin groups. No other reagents, safe and environmental protection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed method for determination of animal vitamin B12 injection in real samples, the found was the value of three parallel experiments.

head.ce:title: A yellow-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing of vitamin B12 and their cell-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomaterials Research Center, School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biomaterials Research Center, School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zishan Sun and Zikang Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple approach for the synthesis of novel yellow luminescent carbon dots (Y-CDs) has been developed via solvothermal method using l-tartaric acid and urea as precursors. The obtained Y-CDs were found to emit bright yellow luminescence under UV illumination with a quantum yield of 16.7%. Meanwhile, the Y-CDs exhibited excitation-dependence, photo-stability, and well dispersibility. The Y-CDs efficiently and selectively detected the VB12 in aqueous solution through a fluorescent “Turn Off” phenomenon. Excellent linear relationship between Y-CDs emission ratio and VB12 concentration from 0 μM to 200 μM is observed, in which the correlation coefficient is 0.997 and the detection limit is 2.045 μmol/L. The quenching mechanism was investigated through proper microscopic and spectroscopic studies. In a parallel experiment, the Y-CDs were used as a cell-imaging probe for MDA-MB-231 cells. Our results demonstrated that as-obtained Y-CDs can be simply used as versatile probes, highlighting their potential in different biological studies.

A novel N-doped yellow-emitting carbon dots (Y-CDs) for VB12 sensing and cellular imaging were synthesized via solvothermal method. The Y-CDs displays a yellow emission at 550 nm (QY = 16.7%) and exhibits a high-selective and well-linear relationship between integrated intensity and VB12 concentration in 0–200 μmol/L range. Furthermore, Y-CDs possess good bio-compatibility and can be used for cell imaging as excellent fluorescence probes.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of emerging luminescent nanomaterial, great attentions have been paid to carbon dots (CDs), due to its excellent luminescent property [1], facile synthesis [2], easy surface functionalization [3], good biocompatibility with low toxicity [4,5]. At present, CDs is mainly used for bioimaging [6], electrochemical biosensor [7], inkjet printing dye [8], drug detection [9], metal ion detection [10], etc. Up to date, various CDs have been reported, but the majority of them emit strong blue emission under ultraviolet excitation [11,12]. Since the Stokes shifts of blue-emitting CDs is small, severe interference between the scattering spectrum and its emission spectrum is expected. Besides, the blue autofluorescence of biological matrix could interfere the detection of CDs fluorescence signal [13,14]. Therefore, it is greatly desirable to obtain novel long-wavelength-emitting CDs (i.e. yellow/orange/red/near-IR light). However, compared with widely-investigated blue-emitting CDs, the reports of long-wavelength-emitting CDs are quite rare, while their photoluminescent (PL) quantum yields (QYs) are low and the corresponding synthetic routes are complicate. So, it is quite a great challenge to synthesis novel CDs with long-wavelength-emission and high quantum yield for sensing and bio-imaging application.

Vitamins, as a kind of organic substances, which is necessary for humans and animals to keep regular physiological functions. Vitamins B12, also called as cyanocobalamin, belongs to the cobalamin family of compounds, which could be taken by mouth for proper function of nervous system and brain, as well as red blood cell formation [15]. Recent studies have also shown that serum VB12 levels are associated with hearing loss in humans [16]. Besides, adequate VB12 and folate guard against the adverse effects of DDT (Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane) on subclinical early pregnancy and clinical pregnancy [17]. In the human body, a variety of symptoms occur such as headache and nausea if VB12 is lower than the normal level. Furthermore, the lack of VB12 may lead to pernicious anemia and increases the risk of heart disease which will endanger human life [18,19]. Hence, diverse methods have been carried out to detect the VB12 sensitively and accurately, such as capillary zone electrophoresis, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), absorption spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [20–22] etc. However, these methods possess some disadvantages like high cost, complex operation, time consuming that may limit their applications. Table .1 shows the advantages and disadvantages of different methods for detecting VB12 [23,24]. Compared with other methods, CDs-based luminescent sensing VB12 through inner filter effect (IFE) has some advantages.

In this paper, we synthesized a new yellow-emitting CDs via solvothermal synthesis method using tartaric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen source. The YCDs possess good fluorescent stability at various pH and temperature conditions, large Stokes shift (4040 cm−1) and high QYs (16.7%). The quenching mechanism of Y-CDs’ luminescent sensing of VB12 is found to be inner filter effect, while the detection of VB12 in real injection samples shows good recovery result. In addition, the Y-CDs were used as the fluorescent imaging agent in MDA-MB-231 cells. It is found that Y-CDs were successfully enter into the cell nucleus with satisfactory imaging results. The results indicate that the obtain Y-CDs might be applied as a promising multifunctional fluorescent probe for cell imaging and detecting VB12.

l-tartaric acid, urea, anhydrous dimethyl formamide (DMF), ethanol absolute, acetone, methanol, PBS basic (pH = 7.4), vitamin B12 (VB12), fetal bovine serum (FBS), animal vitamin B12 injection, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), paraformaldehyde (4%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the yellow-emitting carbon dots (Y-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 2.0 g l-tartaric acid and 1.0 g urea were dispersed in 10 mL DMF and followed by 5 min ultrasonic treatment. Then, the mixture was put into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. Then, 20 mL ethanol was added into the brown solution and then centrifuged for 5 min at 8000 rpm to remove the precipitate. The Y-CDs is obtained after removing the solvent by reduced pressure distillation.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (ESCALAB 250Xi). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded by PerKin EImer spectrometer. transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed by Tecnai F20 field emission microscope and Brucker D8 X-ray diffractometer, respectively. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was collected by Metash UV-5200 spectrophotometer. Luminescent spectra and decay lifetime data were measured by Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer. Light source of FLS980 spectrometer for PL/PLE and decay lifetime measurement is Xe900 and nF900 lamp, respectively. The power of Xe900 and nF900 lamp is 450 W and 150 W flash, and the corresponding spectral range is 200–2000 nm and 220–800 nm. The pulse width of nF900 is 1.0–1.6 ns, while Xe900 is a continuous wave lamp. Quantum yield data was obtained by the method of Ref. [25]. Cellular imaging was performed by Carl-Zeiss LSM700 confocal laser fluorescence microscopy.

The determination of VB12 was performed at room temperature. Briefly, Y-CDs (6 mg/mL, 100 μL), PBS (2100 μL), and various concentrations of 800 μL VB12 water solutions were added to a cuvette, and the corresponding emission spectra were measured at 450 nm excitation. For analysis of real samples, various amounts of vitamin B12 were spiked to the animal vitamin B12 injection (QUANYU bio-tech company, 0.50 mg/mL), the samples were detected according to the above procedure. Each test was repeated three times and then calculated the corresponding recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD).

MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 1✕105/mL density, 100 μL medium (DMEM containing 10% FBS) is added per well. After 24 h incubation at 37 °C with 5% CO2, 100 mL of medium containing various concentrations of Y-CDs was added to the designated wells after discarded the original medium. The controls were treated with medium only. Then, after another 24 h, 10 μL of CCK-8 was added into every well and culture for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, a microplate reader (Biotek, USA) is used for measuring the absorbance at 450 nm.

MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded into a glass bottom cell culture dish and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, the same concentrations of Y-CDs were separately added in each plate. After cultivate for different times (1 and 4 h), the supernatant was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS and then refilled with 4% paraformaldehyde. Finally, it was stained with DAPI for further characterization. Images were captured with a confocal microscope, where the emission intensity of Y-CDs was recorded at 488 nm excitation.

The structure morphology, surface function groups as well as the chemical composition in the Y-CDs is characterized by XPS, FT-IR, XRD and TEM. The wide-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 a) show three major binding energy peaks at 285.3, 399.1, and 532.1 eV for C(1s), N(1s) and O(1s), respectively. As seen in Fig. 1b, c and d, three sub-peaks can be decomposed from the C (1s) peaks (Fig. 1b) at 285.3 eV (C–C/CC), 285.9 eV (C–N), and 289.7 eV (CO). Moreover, the CO and N–O binding is verified by the binding peaks at 533.0 eV in high-resolved O(1s) XPS spectrum (Fig. 1c). The typical peak at 401.2 eV is assigned to C–N binding of amine N. The surface groups of YCDs is further confirmed by FT-IR (Fig. 1e) The strong peak at 3425 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of O–H. N had been successfully doped in Y-CDs due to the appearance of stretching vibration of N–H (3300-3000 cm−1). Moreover, the peaks around 1676 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 belong to the CC and C–O–C bond, respectively. The results showed that Y-CDs contains various functional groups. It is well-known that nitrogen heteroatoms will enhance the luminescence of Y-CDs, i.e. both free electrons and holes can be introduced by nitrogen doping. Thus, the electron transfer in π-conjugated domains is enhanced and the probability of radiative recombination increases, which eventually results in an efficient emission [26].

The XRD pattern (Fig. 2 a) shows a broad reflection at 2θ = 23.34°, corresponding to the interlayer spacing of (002) graphite structure. TEM image (Fig. 2b) illustrate their spherical shapes and relatively monodisperse sizes, with diameters of ~8.20 nm. The results show that the cell spacing is 0.210 nm, which is close to the surface layer of graphite carbon (002) and coincides with the X-ray diffraction pattern.

The absorption, excitation and emission spectra of Y-CDs can be seen in Fig. 2c. The π-π* transition of the CC and CN bond is responsible for the adsorption band at < 300 nm region. while the absorption band in 350–500 nm region is assigned to the n-π* and the π-π* transitions from the CO bond and aromatic fluorophore structure. The bandgap transition at 450 nm is originated from interfacial excitation on the surface of the Y-CDs. At optimal 450 nm excitation, the Y-CDs solution shows a peak wavelength at 550 nm which displays a yellow emission band. The QY of yellow emission was determined by employing a sodium fluorescence emission standard, and calculated to be 16.7% and 4.5% for Y-CDs dispersed in ethanol and deionized water, respectively. Chemically speaking, hydrogen bonding is stronger in ethanol than in water, so the molecular dipole moment further affects the surface electron state, resulting in higher quantum efficiency of carbon points in ethanol. Thus, the following luminescent property measurements were carried out in an CD ethanol solution. The inset photos showed the bright yellow of Y-CDs under excited at 395 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The emitting-color of Y-CDs can be recognized from the CIE chromaticity diagrams with the corresponding CIE coordinates of (0.390, 0.525) (Fig. 2d). Y-CDs have a large Stokes shift of 4040 cm−1, which could help to reduce the interference caused by probe self-absorption.

Because of multiple luminescent centers in carbon dots, their excitation-dependent PL properties are deemed as the special characteristics [27,28]. The emission peak gradually shifts from 510 to 575 nm with increasing from 400 to 500 nm excitation (Fig. 2e). The excitation-dependent emission property is probably attributed to the different luminescence centers or radiative contributions from the surface states in Y-CDs. The Y-CDs emission supposed to be quenched in the aggregate state due to the aggregation-induced quenching effect (Fig. 3 a). It is found that the maximum emission is obtained when excited at 450 nm and results in a bright yellowish-green emission with peak at 550 nm. Moreover, with further increasing of excitation wavelength, the corresponding emission intensity decreases gradually (Fig. 2f).

The Y-CDs was placed in different conditions including pH, temperature and ionic strengths to explore the fluorescence stability. Fig. S1a showed that the pH increased from 0.47 to 4.10 leads to the increase of fluorescence intensity, but reminds stable the pH from 4.10 to 7.27, and then continue increase with the pH from 8.95 to 10.72. The emission wavelength remains almost unchanged at various pH conditions, which demonstrates that the Y-CDs are relatively stable in polar environments.

The interesting Y-CDs characteristics are solvent-dependent and concentration-dependent emission. As shown in Fig. S1b, the solvent polarity has significant influence on the Y-CDs luminescence. When the solvent polarity increases from acetone to water, the yellow peak red shifts from 525 nm to 550 nm with varied emission intensity. The rearrangement of Y-CDs surface electron caused by the increase of solvent polarity, which resulting in the large change of dipole moment [29]. Apparently, the emission wavelength gradually red shift with the solvent polarity become greater according to the Lippert equation [30]. As seen in Fig. 3a, as the Y-CDs concentration increases from 2.5 mg/mL to 150 mg/mL, a red-shift of emission peak wavelength from 550 nm to 605 nm is observed, which is due to the energy gap shrinkage by Y-CDs aggregation at high concentration. Besides, the emission intensity of Y-CDs rapidly decreased when the concentration was expressly high, which is attributed to the decreasing distances and increasing interactions between molecules, several mechanisms like aggregation-induced quenching, emission reabsorption, inner filter effect, and collisional quenching could be responsible [31]. An obvious color change from yellow to red can be observed by naked eye and shown in Fig. 3b, i.e. the calculated CIE coordinates shift from yellow (0.396, 0.531) to red (0.544, 0.450) with increasing concentration. The graphs of Y-CDs ethanol solutions with different concentrations at 450 nm excitation (Fig. 3c) clearly exhibit the similar tendency.

The emission thermal stability of luminescent materials is a vital parameter for their applications [32–37]. Since carbon dots have similar characteristics in ethanol and water, but the fluorescence intensity in ethanol is better, so ethanol is used as solvent in the quantum efficiency experiment. The Y-CDs (6 mg/mL) was dissolved in ethanol and measured under 450 nm excitation ranging from 20 to 65 °C. As shown in Fig. S2a, the emission intensity held steady when the temperature goes up to 35 °C. Then, it decreased slightly as the temperature rises, i.e. 84.7% of the room temperature (RT) intensity at 65 °C. Fig. S2b demonstrates that the ionic strength-dependent emission spectra of Y-CDs. The emission intensity of Y-CDs remains unchangeable even in a high ionic strength condition of 1.2 M NaCl. The result indicates that Y-CDs could be stable in a medium with high ionic strength and not to be aggregate. The results indicate that the Y-CDs exhibit promising stability and could be used as a good fluorescence probe.

The measured broad emission band (Fig. S3a) can be decomposed into two sub-peaks using Gaussian fitting, which are centered at 18,382 cm−1 (544 nm) and 16,667 cm−1 (600 nm) suggesting the existence of at least two emission centers. To confirm the existence of multiple energy levels, the emission-wavelength-dependent decay curves at 405 nm excitation were obtained. As shown in Fig. S3b, all lifetimes are well fitted to a decay model obeying a double exponential function. There are six independent decay lifetimes at 4.91, 5.38, 5.82, 6.04, 6.04, 6.21, 6.09 and 5.90 ns for λ em = 480, 500, 520, 540, 560, 580, 600 and 600 nm, respectively. The different decay lifetime of Y-CDs monitoring different emission wavelength further confirms that those emissions sub-bands are from different emission centers.

Taking the advantages above, Y-CDs can be utilized as a kind of carrier to loading drug or as a fluorescent sensor to detecting medicines in the cells. Due to the VB12 are easily decomposition in a strong acid (pH < 2) or an alkaline solution, VB12 detection is carried out in the PBS (pH = 7.4). Fig. 4 a depicted the change of emission intensity of the Y-CDs at different concentration of VB12 (0, 2, 5, 7, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 120, 150, 175, 200 μM). It is obvious that with the VB12 concentration increases, the emission intensity of Y-CDs decreases gradually. It is worth mentioning that the emission peak splits into two with the concentration increasing from 100 μM, which is due to the spectral overlap of VB12 absorption and Y-CDs 544 nm emission sub-peak (Fig. 5 a). As can be seen from Fig. S3a, the emission peak of YCD can be divided into 544 nm and 600 nm peaks through gaussian decomposition, indicating that the YCD has at least two luminous centers. In Fig. 5, the absorption peak of VB12 overlaps with the peak of 544 nm, therefore the internal filtration effect mainly occurs between VB12 and the peak of 544 nm. Eventually lead to the emission peak of YCD is gradually splitting into two peaks. Furthermore, Fig. 4b showed the calibration curve for determination of VB12. The relationship between emission intensity of Y-CDs and VB12 concentration could be well described by the Stern-Volmer equation I/I
0 = - 0.0208[c] + 0.9945, where I 0 is the emission intensity of Y-CDs in absence of VB12, and I is that at presence of VB12, c is the concentration of VB12 (μM). The linear range is from 5 to 200 μM with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was 2.045 μM based on 3 K/s. More importantly, the emitting color turn from yellowish-green to dark under 395 nm UV illumination, when concentration of 0 μM VB12 and 200 μM VB12 is added into Y-CDs solution, that can be obviously observed by naked eye (Fig. 4a inset).

Related experiments were done to figure out the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The spectral overlap is observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of VB12 and the emission spectrum of Y-CDs (Fig. 5a). As a result, either inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) might be responsible for fluorescence quenching mechanism [38]. The difference between them is IFE is the attenuation or absorption of emission radiation by quencher of excited beam in solution. The second absorber simply filters out the emission of particles, so it will not change the absorption peak and fluorescence lifetime of CDs [39]. In order to further figure out the exact quenching mechanism, the decay lifetime variation depending on VB12 concentration was measured and shown in Fig. 5b. It is found that after add the VB12, the fluorescence lifetime of Y-CDs remained unchanged, which revealed that the Y-CDs’ quenching mechanism is IFE rather than FRET. IFE is a radiation energy conversion process, which is based on absorption of the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorophores by absorbers.

To further confirm the IFE quenching effect and the Y-CDs selectivity, several UV–vis absorption spectra of different vitamins, including thiamine (VB1), nicotinic acid (VB3), (R)-pantothenic acid (VB5), ascorbic acid (VC), and Vitamin E (VE) are displayed in Fig. 6 a. It is obvious that only the UV–vis absorption spectrum of VB12 have the significant overlap with the emission spectrum of Y-CDs. Fig. 6b shows that the potentially interfering had few or no interference on the fluorescence quenching response to VB12. The results demonstrate that the built method has a high selectivity toward VB12.

In order to verify the feasibility of above-mentioned method in sensing actual samples, the experiment was done to detect the animal vitamin B12 injection (0.5 mg/mL). The operating steps were conducted in the same conditions and the results were presented in Table .2 . The recoveries of VB12 was rose from 96.9% to 100.2% with RSD range from 1.1% to 5.8%. The results show that it’s feasible to detect VB12 in practical samples.

The CCK-8 assay was assessed to analyze the toxicity of Y-CDs using MDA-MB-231 cells as the target before cell imaging experiments by referring Ref. [40–42]. After cultivating the cells for 24 h with variable Y-CDs concentrations, it's apparent that the Y-CDs show minor or no toxicity to the cells. (Fig. 7 a). These finding indicate that the Y-CDs have outstanding biocompatibility and low toxicity therefore are fit to do cell imaging.

Owing to the low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the Y-CDs were employed for imaging in MDA-MB-231 cells. To further locate the cells and identify whether Y-CDs has entered the nucleus, DAPI staining was performed to assess morphological characteristics. The MDA-MB-231 cells were incubated with the 20 mg/mL Y-CDs solutions for 1 and 4 h respectively, and then were imaged through a confocal microscope which under 405 nm and 488 nm laser excitation. As shown in Fig. 7b, after 1 h incubation, blue emission of DAPI is found at 405 nm excitation, while the yellow emission intensity of Y-CDs in cell nucleus were weak, indicated that the Y-CDs has not been enter the cell nucleus in short time. Furthermore, the yellow fluorescence is significantly increased and clearly observed in cell nucleus after 4 h incubation. These results showed that Y-CDs possess so excellent biocompatibility that can penetrate the cell membrane into cell nucleus and monitor VB12 concentration in live cells. Therefore, the Y-CDs are expected to have some applications in biology.

In this study, we have prepared the Y-CDs from tartaric acid and urea via a green and simple solvothermal method for the first time. The Y-CDs show an average size of ~8 nm, narrow size distribution and high pH & thermal stability, while demonstrate strong excitation-dependent yellow emission at 550 nm with a 16.7% quantum yield. The as-prepared Y-CDs were found to sensitively and selectively detect VB12 and the corresponding mechanism is found to be inner filter effect (IFE). Excellent linear relationship between Y-CDs emission ratio and VB12 concentration from 0 μM to 200 μM is observed, in which the correlation coefficient is 0.997 and the detection limit is 2.045 μmol/L. The feasibility of the method was verified by the determination of VB12 in real samples. Moreover, MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with Y-CDs can emit a yellow fluorescence clearly when they were exposed at a 488 nm excitation, revealing that significant imaging depths achieved with Y-CDs combined with their excellent bio-compatibility and the Y-CDs size are small enough to enter the nucleus. Therefore, the Y-CDs can be a promising multifunctional fluorescent probe for VB12 sensing and cellular imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “Luminescence of yellow-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their applications for VB12 sensing and cell-imaging”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was funded by Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (No. 2018GXNSFBA281200) and Open project of College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201925).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared photoluminescent polymer-carbon nanodots with two-photon fluorescence
Adv Mater

Talanta

Microchim Acta

Mater Res Bull

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly Fe3+-selective fluorescent nanoprobe based on ultrabright N/P codoped carbon dots and its application in biological samples
